turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg has our opinion this morning in case 16-399, Perry versus The Merit Systems Protection Board.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Under the Civil Service Reform Act, the Merit Systems Protection Board, initialized MSPB, may adjudicate employee complaints of serious personnel actions, for example, their termination or suspension for more than 14 days. This case concerns the judicial forum to which a federal employee should resort when she wishes to appeal a decision of the MSPB. If an employee's complaint about serious adverse action asserts rights under civil service law only, all agree the MSPB decision is reviewable exclusively in The U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit. In contrast, if the employee asserts rights under federal antidiscrimination law only, again, all agree the proper forum for judicial review is a federal district court. Sometimes, an employee invokes both civil service law and antidiscrimination law. She complains of wrongful termination or suspension, or for example, and it attributes that adverse action in whole or in part to discrimination based on her race, gender, age, or disability. That kind of complaint in which a claim for unlawful discrimination is embedded within a complaint challenging an adverse action is descriptively called a mixed case. Five years ago, in a case called Kloeckner against Solis, we held that when the MSPB dismisses a mixed case on the merits or on procedural grounds without reaching the merits, review authority lies in district court, not in the federal circuit. In today's case, we decide that the same review route is in order when the MSPB dismisses a mixed case neither on the merits nor on a procedural ground, but instead for lack of jurisdiction. Anthony Perry, the complaining employee here, received notice of his termination by his employer, the U.S. Census Bureau, for spotty attendance. Perry and the Bureau thereafter subscribed to a settlement in which Perry submitted to a 30-day suspension and early retirement. After retiring, Perry appealed his suspension and retirement to the MSPB, alleging discrimination and retaliation for complaining about discrimination. The settlement, Perry maintained, did not stand in his way, because the Bureau had coerced him into signing it. An MSPB administrative law judge determined that Perry had failed to prove coercion in entering into the settlement. Observing that actions to which an employee consents are non-appealable to the MSPB, the administrative law judge ruled that the Board lacked jurisdiction to entertain Perry's claims. The MSPB affirmed. Perry sought review in the D.C. Circuit, the wrong place. The D.C. Circuit held that the proper review forum was the federal circuit and transferred the case there. We now hold that federal district court, not the federal circuit, is the proper reviewing court when the MSPB dismisses a mixed case on jurisdictional grounds, just as a district court would be the right place if the dismissal rested on the merits or on procedural grounds. Kloeckner derived from the Civil Service Reform Act a clear ruling, mixed cases shall be filed in district court. The key to district court review, the Court explained, is the employee's claim that an agency action appealable to the MSPB violates an antidiscrimination statute. Perry complained of a personal action – personnel action serious enough to appeal to the MSPB a 30-day suspension and forced retirement. And he alleged that the adverse action was based on discrimination. A paradigm mixed case. District court jurisdiction was therefore proper in his case, just as it was in Kloeckner. The government urges the distinction between MSPB merits and procedural decisions on the one hand and the Board's jurisdictional rulings on the other. We find the distinction unwarranted and unworkable. Perry's jurisdiction and merits claims, we point out, are tightly intertwined. Moreover, typing a dismissal jurisdictional rather than procedural or merits-related can be problematic. Further, the MSPB sometimes rules on alternative grounds, calling one ground jurisdictional, the other procedural or substantive. Or the board, considering multiple claims, might dismiss one for want of jurisdiction, another for a procedural lapse, yet another on substantive grounds. Where, in such cases, would they appeal run? In sum, for reasons stated out in the Court's opinion, we hold that the Civil Service Reform Act does not direct the appeals splitting bifurcation the government analysis invites. Instead, in mixed cases such as Perry's in which an employee or former employee complains of serious adverse action prompted in whole or in part by the employing agency's violation of federal antidiscrimination law, the district court is the proper forum for judicial review. We therefore reserve the judgment of the D.C. Circuit. Justice Gorsuch has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justice Thomas joins.
